extracts.extract: OpenRefine's clustering function uses algorithms to identify similar text values and standardize them. After clicking Merge Selected & Re-Cluster, Refine standardizes all of those variations. We'd go through each of these clusters, then apply all of the other methods and keying functions.

Many government agencies publish data tables inside PDFs. In order to work with the tables, we must first liberate them. Cometdocs and Zamzar are two proven services. Sometimes one does better than the other, depending on how the PDF is structured. So try both in real-life situations. Note that conversions will not work with PDFs that were created by scanning other documents.

For students and professionals working with data, cleaning is an essential part of the process. This chapter showed how you can get started to solve some of the most vexing problems with Excel, OpenRefine and online PDF converters.
When we work with data, we will often compare numbers, because raw numbers hardly ever are meaningful by themselves. We create meaning when we compare them to other numbers or benchmarks.

When we work with data, we often generate what are called summary statistics. Some examples are counts, sums and averages. These numbers are used to take snapshots or descriptions of our data. We can generate these easily and they will tell us much about our data.

Outliers, or numbers that are notably low or high, can skew the mean. In those instances, the median is a more accurate reflection of the central tendency. Numbers with an unlimited cap—such as incomes or home sale prices—are more prone to having outliers.

In our analytical toolbox we have a number of comparisons that are easy to generate: amount change or raw change, percent change, percent of total, ratios and rates. The amount change doesn't say much because we lack context. That's why we always want to compare the raw change to the starting point.

Rates are helpful tools because they allow us to more fairly compare numbers from areas with dissimilar populations. When we calculate rates, it's a two-step process. First, we calculate a per capita rate. Then, we multiply that by a standard number to normalize our results. Per capita rates are usually so small that they're fractions and don't make much sense. So we normally multiply them by a standard number.

We can have internal and external benchmarks. Internal benchmarks are numbers that are contained within our data. Percent change could be considered an external benchmark because we're comparing a number from one year to that number in the previous year. Reports created by government agencies, or nongovernmental organizations using data, can be a benchmarking tool.

Ratios are helpful when we want to calculate proportions that show differences between numbers. To generate a ratio we take our number and divide it by another number to which we are drawing a comparison.

Sorting in Excel can be tricky because you first need to highlight all of the data you want to sort, including the headers but excluding any summary functions calculated at the bottom of the sheet. If you fail to highlight all of your data, Excel may sort some of your columns and leave others unsorted. This is a big problem, because you'll have mismatched data.

We used Excel's pivot table function to run simple integrity checks that gave us a better understanding of the values in each of our data columns. Here, we use pivot tables to answer 'How much?' or 'How many?' questions about categories that we find in our data.
A filter is a temporary view of data in Excel. If we want to create a permanent copy we can copy our filtered results and then paste them into a new sheet.

Visualization is simply the act of creating charts based on data. Data analysts will often create many different visualizations that can help show the data from different perspectives, or they will show a subset of data. The techniques of exploratory data analysis, as promoted by statistician Tukey, use different types of graphs to get a better understanding.

The first goal of an infographic is not to be beautiful just for the sake of eye appeal, but above all, to be understandable first, and beautiful after that; or to be beautiful because of its exquisite functionality. Graphics reveal data.

Tufte's theory of graphical excellence instructs us to: show the data; induce the viewer to think about the substance rather than methodology; avoid distorting what the data have to say; present many numbers in a small space; make large data sets coherent; encourage the eye to compare different pieces of data; reveal the data at several levels of detail; serve a reasonably clear purpose; be closely integrated with statistical and verbal descriptions.

Give your chart a title. Label your chart elements including axes and legends. Include a source line—it shows others where you got the data, because you want to give others the ability to try to replicate or even challenge your results. Display enough data to provide context; showing only three years of unemployment data might mask the fact that the rate had been rising in earlier years.

Which chart will be the most appropriate and communicate the best? The answer depends on the kind of data that we want to visualize. Pie charts are best for showing proportions of the whole. Vertical column charts are ideal for showing change over time with discrete time-series data. Line charts are a great choice for continuous time-series data. Scatterplots help us show whether we might have a relationship between two variables.
Avoid the temptation to use 3-D pie charts because they can distort results and make some slices appear bigger than they ought to be.

A map is often the best way to visualize geographic data. Excel is unable to create maps but can do so using plug-in programs. We can use geographic information system programs, such as ArcGIS or Quantum GIS, to create data maps. We can also use online programs, such as Google Fusion Tables.
We prefer to use data based on real dollars because doing so takes into account the effects of inflation. The title also signals to viewers they are looking at data spanning the years 2000 to 2013.

The scatterplot includes a linear trend line that shows the central tendency of the data. Any point that's well above the trend line represents a city whose violent crime numbers are higher than expected. Excel uses a statistical calculation called linear regression to determine the position of the linear trend line.
Sparklines are intense, simple, word-sized graphics that are placed inside individual cells. A sparkline is an excellent tool for helping compare patterns in large data sets.

When evaluating Web-based data visualization platforms, consider that you often surrender control of your data when you make it available on these sites. Make sure that you read the terms of service and the privacy statement. A related risk is that the visualization platform could be taken offline completely.

Database managers, such as Microsoft Access and the open source MySQL, are designed to work with large files. Some database programs can easily work with millions of rows of data, something that's impossible with Excel's default installation settings. Database managers are especially powerful because they allow us to extract specific pieces of data that interest us.

Anyone who learns SQL on one database manager can apply that knowledge to another. Anyone who learns SQL can talk intelligently to database administrators at government agencies, making it easier to negotiate for offline data. Learning SQL forces database users to think logically about their data and how they are structured.

Database managers allow us to link tables together through a process called joining. If we get a database from a government agency that's stored in more than one table, we can stitch the data together to better make sense of it.

Most advanced data analysts prefer to use more robust standalone statistical programs, such as IBM's SPSS, SAS or the open source R. Statistical programs can generate descriptive statistics and run tests that allow us to see whether there's any statistical relationship between variables. These are called inferential statistics.
In medical and social science research, analysts will often run descriptive statistics first to understand their data. Then they will run inferential statistics to test their hypotheses.
Refine can also split and merge values in columns. It even has its own scripting language called GREL, which allows you to write code for cleaning data.
Anyone who wants to work with data needs to have some basic tools: spreadsheets, text editors and data cleaner. OpenRefine should work comfortably with data files of up to 10 columns by 100,000 rows.
Practice safe computing by saving a working copy of the file using File Save As, using a new file name.

Experienced data users typically use database manager programs, such as Microsoft Access and MySQL, to create groups and summaries. However, we can perform many of the same functions using spreadsheets.
